questions.question: Pleural effusion is often associated with which malignancy?
options: A. Skin cancer
B. Lung cancer
C. Bone cancer
D. Thyroid cancer
questions.rationale: Pleural effusion is frequently associated with lung cancer, and mediastinal cancer specifically carries a higher incidence of it.

questions.question: Which cancer type carries a particularly higher incidence of pleural effusion?
options: A. Skin cancer
B. Mediastinal cancer
C. Prostate cancer
D. Bladder cancer
questions.rationale: Patients with mediastinal cancer have a higher incidence of pleural effusion, an important consideration in oncology patients with dyspnea.

questions.question: An oncology patient on chemotherapy presents with progressive dyspnea. What TWO conditions from earlier chapters should be actively considered alongside disease progression?
options: A. Simple anxiety and dehydration only
B. Pleural effusion and cardiac tamponade (pericardial effusion)
C. Appendicitis and cholecystitis
D. Hypoglycemia and DKA only
questions.rationale: Both pleural effusion and pericardial effusion/tamponade are recognized oncology-related complications that can cause dyspnea, connecting this chapter back to the cardiovascular chapter's Beck's triad content.

questions.question: Why is cancer considered a risk factor for pulmonary embolism?
options: A. It has no relationship to clotting
B. Malignancy is a recognized clotting risk factor, alongside immobility and surgery
C. Cancer only affects bleeding risk, never clotting
D. This relationship applies only to skin cancer
questions.rationale: Malignancy is one of the classic risk factors for venous thromboembolism, making PE an important differential in oncology patients with sudden dyspnea.

questions.question: Why is it a mistake to attribute every new symptom in a cancer patient to 'the cancer'?
options: A. Cancer patients never develop unrelated new problems
B. New symptoms may reflect a different, treatable cause that would be missed by anchoring on the known diagnosis
C. It is always correct to attribute symptoms to the cancer
D. This has no bearing on patient care
questions.rationale: Anchoring on a known diagnosis risks missing a new, unrelated, and potentially treatable cause of the patient's current complaint.

questions.question: What is the key to active listening, as emphasized throughout this course?
options: A. Asking only closed yes/no questions quickly
B. Getting on your patient's level and asking follow-up questions
C. Interrupting frequently to move the interview along
D. Avoiding eye contact to reduce patient anxiety
questions.rationale: Active listening — engaging at the patient's level with genuine follow-up questions — surfaces the nuance needed for an accurate history, especially in complex patients.

questions.question: Why does 'trust YOUR exam' remain an important principle even in complicated, multi-diagnosis patients?
options: A. Your findings become unreliable in complex patients
B. Your own primary assessment findings are a consistent, reliable data source regardless of how complicated the history is
C. Complex patients should only be assessed by specialists
D. This principle only applies to simple patients
questions.rationale: Regardless of how many diagnoses or medications a patient has, your direct findings remain a dependable anchor for clinical decision-making.

questions.question: Which safety/scene principle from the first chapter reappears as a course-wide theme?
options: A. Multiple patients with the same signs and symptoms should prompt inquiry into a shared cause
B. Every scene is automatically safe
C. Only the first patient at a scene needs assessment
D. Scene safety only matters for trauma calls
questions.rationale: The shared-cause pattern-recognition principle applies across every category of medical call covered in this course, not just the foundational chapter.

questions.question: What is the recurring 'skeleton' approach that applies across every chapter in this course?
options: A. A unique, unrelated protocol for every diagnosis
B. Systematic assessment, differential diagnosis from possibilities to probabilities, trusting your exam, active listening, and treating life threats first
C. Skipping the history in favor of only physical exam
D. Treating every complaint identically regardless of findings
questions.rationale: The course's core assessment skeleton — systematic assessment, differential thinking, trusting your exam, active listening, and life-threats-first — recurs across every body-system chapter.

questions.question: An oncology patient presents with decreased breath sounds at one lung base and dullness to percussion, with progressive dyspnea. What should be high on your differential?
options: A. Simple anxiety
B. Pleural effusion
C. Appendicitis
D. Hypoglycemia
questions.rationale: Decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion with progressive dyspnea in an oncology patient are classic findings for pleural effusion, especially given the lung/mediastinal cancer association.

questions.question: Why is unhurried, open-ended history-taking especially valuable in complex, chronically ill patients?
options: A. It wastes valuable field time with no benefit
B. Complex histories with multiple diagnoses and medications hide nuance that only unhurried, open-ended questioning surfaces
C. Complex patients should be assessed with fewer questions, not more
D. It is only useful for pediatric patients
questions.rationale: The more complex the patient's history, the more valuable careful, open-ended listening becomes for finding the detail that changes the differential.

questions.question: Which statement best summarizes the intended takeaway of this capstone chapter?
options: A. Each medical specialty requires an entirely separate, unrelated assessment method
B. The same disciplined, systematic assessment approach applies across every category of medical complaint, from respiratory to oncologic
C. Oncology patients require no differential diagnosis since the cause is always known
D. Active listening and trusting your exam are only relevant to the first chapter
questions.rationale: This chapter ties the course together: the same core assessment principles introduced early apply, undiminished, to every subsequent body-system chapter, including oncologic and complex presentations.

questions.question: A patient with multiple chronic diagnoses presents with a vague 'not feeling right' complaint. What is the recommended approach?
options: A. Assume the most recently diagnosed condition explains everything
B. Apply the systematic assessment approach: history-heavy interview, active listening, wide differential, trust your exam
C. Skip the history since there are too many diagnoses to sort through
D. Treat only for the condition the family suggests
questions.rationale: Complex, multi-diagnosis patients benefit most from the disciplined systematic approach — the framework does not change just because the patient's history is complicated.

questions.question: Why does this course repeatedly return to the same assessment principles (systematic approach, differential diagnosis, active listening, trust your exam) across different body systems?
options: A. Because each chapter actually requires a completely different, unrelated method
B. Because these principles are the actual engine of good field decision-making, regardless of which body system is involved
C. Because repetition is only for memorization, with no clinical purpose
D. Because oncologic patients don't need any of these principles
questions.rationale: The recurring principles are not filler — they are the actual clinical reasoning process, and every chapter's specific facts (drug names, exam findings) are applied inside that same reasoning framework.
